content.text: Fixed-size splitting is blind to meaning — it draws boundaries wherever a token counter hits the limit, potentially severing a sentence in the middle of a key definition. Semantic chunking and structural chunking are two strategies that let the content itself determine where boundaries fall.
places a boundary wherever the meaning shifts, as measured by the cosine similarity between consecutive sentence embeddings.
places boundaries at explicit document landmarks — headings, section fences, function definitions, XML elements — without computing any embeddings.

attrs.alt: Semantic chunking diagram: six source sentences, pairwise cosine similarity chart showing a drop between S3 and S4 below threshold 0.55 triggering a split, and two output chunks. Bottom panel shows structural chunking applied to HTML/Markdown, PDFs with TOC, code, JSON/XML, and when to combine both approaches.

content.text: The algorithm embeds every sentence independently, computes pairwise cosine similarity between adjacent sentences, and splits wherever similarity drops below a threshold
is typically set adaptively as the
: rank all pairwise similarity scores and split at any pair below the Nth percentile. LangChain's

from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

# percentile: split where similarity drops below the Nth percentile
chunker = SemanticChunker(
    embeddings,
    breakpoint_threshold_type="percentile",
    breakpoint_threshold_amount=90,   # 90th percentile → only the sharpest drops
)

text = open("annual_report.txt").read()
docs = chunker.create_documents([text])

print(f"{len(docs)} semantic chunks")
for doc in docs[:3]:
    first = doc.page_content[:80].replace("\n", " ")
    print(f"  [{len(doc.page_content)} chars] {first}...")
Split at pairs below the Nth percentile of all similarity scores
Most documents — adapts to the distribution of similarities in the text
Split where similarity is more than N standard deviations below the mean
Documents with very uniform similarity until topic changes (e.g. encyclopaedias)
Split where similarity falls below Q1 − N × IQR
Outlier-robust; useful when a few sentences are very short or very long
Compute gradient of similarity changes; split on steep drops
Long documents with many subtle topic shifts
The computational overhead is significant. For a document with
embedding calls before any chunks are produced:
A 20-page report might contain 400 sentences. At $0.02 per million tokens with

and ~15 tokens per sentence, that is roughly $0.00012 per document — negligible per document but non-trivial at corpus scale. More importantly, each embedding call adds latency. For incremental ingestion, cache sentence embeddings in a key-value store keyed by sentence hash:

import hashlib, json
from pathlib import Path

CACHE_DIR = Path(".semantic_cache")
CACHE_DIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

def embed_with_cache(sentences: list[str], embed_fn) -> list[list[float]]:
    """Embed sentences, skipping any already cached on disk."""
    results = [None] * len(sentences)
    to_embed, indices = [], []

    for idx, s in enumerate(sentences):
        key = hashlib.sha1(s.encode()).hexdigest()
        cache_file = CACHE_DIR / f"{key}.json"
        if cache_file.exists():
            results[idx] = json.loads(cache_file.read_text())
        else:
            to_embed.append(s)
            indices.append((idx, cache_file))

    if to_embed:
        new_embeds = embed_fn(to_embed)
        for (idx, cache_file), vec in zip(indices, new_embeds):
            cache_file.write_text(json.dumps(vec))
            results[idx] = vec

    return results

When a document carries its own structure — headings, section separators, function definitions — that structure usually reflects the author's own topic boundaries and is cheaper to exploit than recomputing similarity. Structural chunking reads those signals directly.
splits on heading levels, preserving each section as a chunk with its heading hierarchy in the metadata:

from langchain_text_splitters import MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter

headers_to_split_on = [
    ("#",  "h1"),
    ("##", "h2"),
    ("###","h3"),
]
splitter = MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter(
    headers_to_split_on=headers_to_split_on,
    strip_headers=False,   # keep heading text inside the chunk
)

markdown_doc = """
# Annual Report 2025

## Cloud Revenue

Q3 cloud revenue grew 34%...

## Research & Development

R&D spending increased by 22%...
"""
chunks = splitter.split_text(markdown_doc)
for c in chunks:
    print(c.metadata, "|", c.page_content[:60])
# {'h1': 'Annual Report 2025', 'h2': 'Cloud Revenue'} | Q3 cloud revenue ...
# {'h1': 'Annual Report 2025', 'h2': 'Research & Development'} | R&D spending ...
The metadata hierarchy (h1, h2, h3) is preserved in every chunk, so a query for a section can filter by heading level or title using vector-store metadata filters.
For HTML where structure is encoded in tags, extract sections before splitting:
uses language-specific separators that align with syntax boundaries. For Python it tries
in order — falling back to coarser boundaries only when finer ones produce chunks too large:

from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter, Language

python_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_language(
    language=Language.PYTHON,
    chunk_size=512,
    chunk_overlap=0,      # functions rarely need overlap
)

js_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter.from_language(
    language=Language.JS,
    chunk_size=512,
    chunk_overlap=0,
)

with open("utils.py") as f:
    code_text = f.read()

chunks = python_splitter.create_documents([code_text])
for ch in chunks:
    lines = ch.page_content.count("\n") + 1
    print(f"  {lines} lines | starts: {ch.page_content[:40]!r}")
, and others. For code corpora, always use language-aware splitting — splitting mid-function produces embeddings that conflate unrelated logic.

The most robust pipeline uses structure first, then semantic splitting within each structural section. This avoids embedding all sentences in a large corpus upfront while still preventing giant sections from becoming single unwieldy chunks:

from langchain_text_splitters import (
    MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter,
    RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter,
)
from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
import tiktoken

enc = tiktoken.encoding_for_model("text-embedding-3-small")
tok = lambda text: len(enc.encode(text))

# Step 1: structural split at headings
header_splitter = MarkdownHeaderTextSplitter(
    headers_to_split_on=[("#", "h1"), ("##", "h2"), ("###", "h3")],
)

# Step 2: semantic splitter for sections > 1024 tokens
embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
semantic_splitter = SemanticChunker(
    embeddings,
    breakpoint_threshold_type="percentile",
    breakpoint_threshold_amount=85,
)

# Step 3: token-based fallback for anything still oversized
fallback_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
    chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64, length_function=tok,
)

def hybrid_split(markdown_text: str):
    structural_chunks = header_splitter.split_text(markdown_text)
    final = []
    for sc in structural_chunks:
        size = tok(sc.page_content)
        if size <= 512:
            final.append(sc)                       # already small enough
        elif size <= 2048:
            subs = semantic_splitter.create_documents([sc.page_content])
            for sub in subs:
                sub.metadata.update(sc.metadata)   # inherit heading metadata
            final.extend(subs)
        else:
            subs = fallback_splitter.split_documents([sc])
            final.extend(subs)
    return final
Structure → Semantic → Token is the three-tier fallback. Most sections stay structural; only oversized ones get semantic refinement; only sections where semantics also fails get the token fallback.
High — S embedding calls per document
Threshold tuning required; slow on large corpora
Reports, docs sites, wikis with clear headings
Fails on poorly structured or heading-free PDFs
Only works if language is detected correctly
Large structured docs with variable section length
Medium — only oversized sections need embeddings
More code to maintain; two configs to tune
Any document type when latency matters

You can score the semantic coherence of a set of chunks without a labelled evaluation set. Compute the mean intra-chunk cosine similarity across all sentence pairs within each chunk — a high value means the chunk holds together well:

import numpy as np
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
import nltk

nltk.download("punkt_tab", quiet=True)
from nltk.tokenize import sent_tokenize

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

def chunk_cohesion(text: str) -> float:
    """Mean pairwise cosine similarity of sentences within a chunk. Range [−1, 1]."""
    sentences = sent_tokenize(text)
    if len(sentences) < 2:
        return 1.0   # single sentence is perfectly cohesive
    vecs = np.array(embeddings.embed_documents(sentences))
    sim_matrix = cosine_similarity(vecs)
    # upper triangle only (no diagonal)
    n = len(sentences)
    upper = [sim_matrix[i, j] for i in range(n) for j in range(i + 1, n)]
    return float(np.mean(upper))

# Compare strategies
from langchain_text_splitters import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker

text = open("sample.txt").read()

fixed_chunks = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(chunk_size=512, chunk_overlap=64).split_text(text)
semantic_chunks_docs = SemanticChunker(embeddings).create_documents([text])
semantic_chunks = [d.page_content for d in semantic_chunks_docs]

fixed_score    = np.mean([chunk_cohesion(c) for c in fixed_chunks])
semantic_score = np.mean([chunk_cohesion(c) for c in semantic_chunks])

print(f"Fixed-size cohesion:  {fixed_score:.3f}")
print(f"Semantic cohesion:    {semantic_score:.3f}")
# Semantic typically scores 0.05–0.15 higher on mixed-topic documents

Track this metric during index-build and add it to each chunk's metadata. A chunk with cohesion below 0.5 is a candidate for re-splitting or flagging as a boundary zone, which the retriever can use to prefer nearby chunks with higher cohesion when assembling context.

When tuning the semantic chunker threshold it helps to visualise the similarity profile of a document before committing to a setting. The following script prints the similarity profile and proposed boundaries so you can see what the splitter will do before you rebuild the full index:

import numpy as np
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
import nltk

nltk.download("punkt_tab", quiet=True)
from nltk.tokenize import sent_tokenize

embeddings = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")

def inspect_boundaries(text: str, threshold_pct: int = 90) -> None:
    """Print the similarity profile and proposed split points."""
    sentences = sent_tokenize(text)
    if len(sentences) < 2:
        print("Too short — only one sentence detected")
        return

    vecs = np.array(embeddings.embed_documents(sentences))
    sims = [
        float(cosine_similarity([vecs[i]], [vecs[i + 1]])[0][0])
        for i in range(len(vecs) - 1)
    ]

    threshold = float(np.percentile(sims, 100 - threshold_pct))
    print(f"Threshold (breakpoint at {threshold_pct}th pct): {threshold:.3f}")
    print(f"{'Pair':>6}  {'Sim':>6}  {'Split?'}")
    print("-" * 30)

    for i, sim in enumerate(sims):
        split = "✂ SPLIT" if sim < threshold else ""
        # Bar chart in ASCII, proportional to similarity
        bar_len = int(sim * 20)
        bar = "█" * bar_len + "░" * (20 - bar_len)
        short_s = sentences[i][:30].replace("\n", " ")
        print(f"S{i}→S{i+1}: {sim:.3f}  {bar}  {split}")
        if split:
            print(f"         → end of: {short_s!r}")

inspect_boundaries(open("sample.txt").read(), threshold_pct=90)

Run this on 5–10 representative documents from the corpus. Look for whether the split points align with natural topic changes. If the splitter is creating boundaries mid-topic (too many splits), raise
. If it is missing obvious shifts (too few splits), lower it.
Adding Structural Context to Semantic Chunks

A semantic chunker does not know which section a sentence came from. When sections share overlapping vocabulary (e.g. an annual report's Risk Factors section and its Forward-Looking Statements section both use finance jargon), the chunk's embedding may not carry enough structural signal for the retriever to distinguish them.
Prepend the nearest ancestor heading to each semantic chunk as a context prefix:

import re
from langchain_core.documents import Document
from langchain_experimental.text_splitter import SemanticChunker
from langchain_openai import OpenAIEmbeddings

_HEADING_RE = re.compile(r"^(#{1,3})\s+(.+)$", re.MULTILINE)

def semantic_chunks_with_headings(
    markdown_text: str,
    embeddings,
    threshold_pct: int = 85,
) -> list[Document]:
    """
    Semantic-chunk a markdown document and inject the nearest heading
    into each chunk's page_content as a prefix.
    """
    # Build a map: character position → heading text
    heading_map = []
    for m in _HEADING_RE.finditer(markdown_text):
        heading_map.append((m.start(), m.group(2).strip()))

    def nearest_heading(pos: int) -> str:
        best = ""
        for h_pos, h_text in heading_map:
            if h_pos <= pos:
                best = h_text
        return best

    chunker = SemanticChunker(
        embeddings,
        breakpoint_threshold_type="percentile",
        breakpoint_threshold_amount=threshold_pct,
    )
    raw_chunks = chunker.create_documents([markdown_text])

    # Inject heading prefix
    out = []
    char_pos = 0
    for raw in raw_chunks:
        heading = nearest_heading(char_pos)
        prefixed = f"[{heading}] {raw.page_content}" if heading else raw.page_content
        out.append(Document(page_content=prefixed, metadata={"section": heading}))
        char_pos += len(raw.page_content)
    return out

emb = OpenAIEmbeddings(model="text-embedding-3-small")
chunks = semantic_chunks_with_headings(open("report.md").read(), emb)
for ch in chunks[:3]:
    print(ch.metadata["section"], "|", ch.page_content[:80])

biases the chunk's embedding toward its section, which improves retrieval precision when two sections share vocabulary. It also surfaces the section title directly in the LLM prompt when the chunk is used as context, making citations more informative.
Too many tiny chunks (low threshold) or one giant chunk (high threshold)
Plot the similarity distribution; pick the natural breakpoint percentile
Code embeddings cluster by token frequency, not logical structure
Use language-aware structural splitting for code
Ignoring document headings when they exist
Unnecessary embedding cost; worse boundary quality
Apply structural splitting first; use semantic only within sections
Re-embedding entire corpus on threshold change
High cost; sentence embeddings are reusable
Cache sentence embeddings; only re-run the similarity thresholding step
